content:
## Root Cause

The error comes from `rustls-native-certs` inside `reqwest` (used by `google-cloud-gax`). When your binary — especially a statically-linked musl build — runs on a system without a system CA store, `rustls` fails to load any trust anchors and refuses to open TLS connections.

```
No CA certificates were loaded from the system
```

## Fix 1 — Bundle WebPKI roots into the binary (recommended)

Cargo unifies features across the entire dependency graph. Adding `rustls-tls-webpki-roots` to your top-level `reqwest` entry causes Mozilla's curated CA bundle (`webpki-roots`) to be compiled into the same `reqwest` that `google-cloud-gax` uses, so the binary carries its own trust anchors and never needs to find them on disk.

```toml
# Cargo.toml
[dependencies]
reqwest = { version = "0.13", features = ["json", "rustls-tls-webpki-roots"] }
```

When both `rustls-tls-native-roots` (pulled in by `google-cloud-gax`) **and** `rustls-tls-webpki-roots` are active, `reqwest` merges both root stores, so the binary still honours local corporate CAs if they exist.

---

## Fix 2 — Install system CA certs on the target host (user-side workaround)

If you cannot rebuild, installing the `ca-certificates` package is enough:

```bash
# Debian / Ubuntu
sudo apt-get install -y ca-certificates

# Alpine Linux
apk add --no-cache ca-certificates

# Or point reqwest at an explicit bundle
export SSL_CERT_FILE=/etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt
```

---

## Fix 3 — Install CA certs during the musl build in CI

Even with the Cargo fix above it is good hygiene to have certs present in the build environment for integration tests or `cargo publish`:

```yaml
# .github/workflows/release.yaml  — inside the build job steps
      - name: Install musl-tools (x86_64-musl)
        if: matrix.target == 'x86_64-unknown-linux-musl'
        run: |
          sudo apt-get update
          sudo apt-get install -y musl-tools ca-certificates   # ← add ca-certificates
```

---

## Why musl static binaries are especially affected

| Build type | CA certificate source |
|---|---|
| `x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu` | Links against glibc, which reads `/etc/ssl/certs` at runtime |
| `x86_64-unknown-linux-musl` | No dynamic libc; `rustls-native-certs` has nothing to parse → **zero roots loaded → TLS fails** |

The `webpki-roots` fix is the only approach that makes the binary self-contained and works identically on Alpine, scratch containers, or any host without a CA bundle installed.
I'm going for fix 3, but would it still work in termux

## Fix 3 Does Not Help in Termux

Fix 3 only installs `ca-certificates` inside the **GitHub Actions runner** (the build machine). It has zero effect on where the compiled binary eventually runs. Nothing gets embedded into the binary itself.

### Why Termux Specifically Breaks

`rustls-native-certs` on Linux probes a hardcoded list of standard paths:

```
/etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt   ← Debian/Ubuntu
/etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt     ← RHEL/Fedora
/usr/lib/ssl/certs/                  ← etc.
```

Termux stores its CA bundle under its own prefix:

```
/data/data/com.termux/files/usr/etc/tls/cert.pem
```

None of the hardcoded paths exist on Android, so `rustls-native-certs` loads **zero roots** and you get the same error.

---

## You Need Fix 1 for Termux to Work

```toml
# Cargo.toml
[dependencies]
reqwest = { version = "0.13", features = ["json", "rustls-tls-webpki-roots"] }
```

This compiles Mozilla's CA bundle directly into the binary. It needs no files on disk at runtime, so it works on:

| Environment | Fix 3 | Fix 1 |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Linux (glibc) | ✅ | ✅ |
| Alpine / musl container | ❌ | ✅ |
| Termux (Android) | ❌ | ✅ |
| Scratch container | ❌ | ✅ |

---

## Keep Fix 3 Anyway

Fix 3 is still worth keeping — it ensures the CI runner itself can reach `crates.io` and other HTTPS endpoints during `cargo build`/`cargo publish` — but pair it with Fix 1 so the **resulting binary** is also self-contained:

```yaml
- name: Install musl-tools (x86_64-musl)
  if: matrix.target == 'x86_64-unknown-linux-musl'
  run: |
    sudo apt-get update
    sudo apt-get install -y musl-tools ca-certificates
```

```toml
reqwest = { version = "0.13", features = ["json", "rustls-tls-webpki-roots"] }
```
